CREST AWARD · STUDENT PORTFOLIO
Shaurya Jain

Mapping the edges
of a growing city, one grid cell at a time.

A satellite-imagery and machine-learning project detecting and predicting informal settlement expansion in Mumbai — built end-to-end, from raw Sentinel-2 data to a tested, honestly-evaluated monitoring pipeline.

View Deliverables GitHub Repository
About This Project

I'm Shaurya Jain, and this is my CREST Award project: an end-to-end pipeline that detects informal settlements in Mumbai from free satellite imagery, and predicts where they're likely to expand next.

The project combines remote sensing, deep learning, and geospatial machine learning — adapting a transformer-based segmentation model to multispectral satellite bands, then building a spatial monitoring framework and classifier on top of its output. Every stage was tested against real, independently sourced evidence rather than assumed to work.

Along the way this project meant learning to work with satellite data platforms, adapt a pretrained neural network to an entirely new kind of input, debug issues spanning hardware backends, file formats, and API version mismatches, and honestly report results that didn't always go the way I expected — including several approaches that made things worse, not better.

Google Earth Engine PyTorch SegFormer Remote Sensing Random Forest GeoPandas / rasterio OpenStreetMap Scientific Writing
Project Journey

From raw satellite pixels to a tested prediction pipeline.

Recorded across every phase of the project, in the order it was actually built.

8 hrs

Background Research

Reviewing existing slum-mapping methods, published benchmarks, and available free data sources.

12 hrs

Data Acquisition Pipeline

Sentinel-2 access via Google Earth Engine, cloud-filtered compositing, ground-truth alignment.

18 hrs

Detection Model Development

Adapting SegFormer to 10-band multispectral input, training, and systematic ablation experiments.

10 hrs

Grid Framework & Features

Designing the 160m spatial monitoring grid and engineering geospatial context features.

9 hrs

Expansion Prediction Model

Training the Random Forest classifier, threshold selection, and validation.

11 hrs

Debugging & Troubleshooting

Resolving hardware, data-format, and API issues encountered at every stage of the pipeline.

7 hrs

Visualization & Outputs

Maps, charts, an interactive summary, and a project poster.

9 hrs

Reporting & Documentation

Written report, results summary, and presentation materials.

Total recorded time: 84 hours

Final Outcomes

What the finished pipeline actually achieves.

0.676
Detection F1
0.510
Detection IoU
0.68
Expansion recall
2 / 6
Verified years
The detection model generalized consistently across two independent years, and the expansion classifier revealed that settlement growth is driven far more by adjacency to existing clusters than by proximity to infrastructure — a finding tested with a dedicated control experiment, not assumed. The full system is presented honestly as a monitoring and prioritization aid, not an authoritative forecasting tool.
Reflections

What this project actually taught me.

Technical

  • Adapting a pretrained neural network to an entirely new kind of input data, rather than only using models off the shelf.
  • Diagnosing root causes methodically — unit mismatches, coordinate-ordering bugs, and file-format issues — rather than guessing at fixes.
  • Building a full geospatial pipeline from raw satellite data through to a tabular machine learning model.

Beyond the Code

  • Reporting negative results honestly, including experiments that made the model worse, rather than hiding them.
  • Thinking carefully about how a tool like this could be misused, and designing its framing to guard against that.
  • Recognizing the real limits of a small student project, and being precise about what it can and cannot claim.
